_note: Marion's STAFF-TRAINING knowledge layer. Distinct from the product catalog (manufacturers/*) — this is the teaching/measurement/material-science knowledge that powers staff certification and lets Marion explain HOW to measure, identify, and select seals. Sourced from the FluidSeal AB Staff Training Hub (caliper module, product-profiles module, and the 6-level certification program). Marion can answer 'how do I…' questions from here and can point staff/customers to the interactive training pages listed in destinations.
source: FluidSeal AB Staff Training Hub (Modules 1–3 + 6-level certification)

destinations._note: Live training pages published from this repo under /training/. Marion links here to hand off to the full interactive module.

anatomy: External (outside) jaws — measure outer diameters, e.g. piston seal OD.
Internal (inside) jaws — measure bores and inner diameters, e.g. rod seal ID.
Scale beam — the main inch/metric graduated bar.
Dial + bezel — fine reading; rotate bezel nut to zero.
Thumb wheel — fine slide control; lock screw / clamp screw to hold a reading.
reading_procedure: Close jaws fully and confirm the dial reads zero; rotate the bezel to zero if not.
Read the whole inches/large increments off the scale beam at the slider edge.
Read the fractional value off the dial (each revolution = 0.1" on a typical inch caliper).
Add beam + dial for the total. Lock the clamp screw before removing the part if needed.

measurement_order.rod_seals: Measure cross-section (c.s.) first, then i.d., o.d., and hgt. The c.s./i.d./o.d./hgt. order is universal for rod seals.
measurement_order.piston_seals: OD (outer diameter) is listed first for piston seals, because it contacts the cylinder bore.
measurement_order.best_practice: Measure the GROOVE (gland), not the old seal — used seals are deformed/worn and give false dimensions.

measurement_rules_qa.q: In what order do I measure a rod seal?
measurement_rules_qa.a: Cross section (c.s.) first, then i.d., o.d., and hgt. — the universal rod-seal order.

measurement_rules_qa.q: For a piston seal, which dimension is listed first?
measurement_rules_qa.a: OD (outer diameter), because it contacts the cylinder bore.

measurement_rules_qa.q: Should I measure the old seal or the groove?
measurement_rules_qa.a: Measure the groove/gland. A used seal is worn and deformed and will give the wrong size.

materials_qa.q: What's the difference between BR.PTFE and GF.PTFE?
materials_qa.a: BR.PTFE (bronze-filled) gives better thermal conductivity and wear resistance for lubricated metallic bores. GF.PTFE (glass-filled) gives better chemical resistance for dry or chemical environments.

materials_qa.q: Why can't standard polyurethane seals run in hot water?
materials_qa.a: Water hydrolyzes the urethane linkages — especially polyester-based AU grade. Use EPDM for water/steam service.

materials_qa.q: What seal works with phosphate ester hydraulic fluid (Skydrol)?
materials_qa.a: IIR (Butyl) or EPDM. Standard NBR and polyurethane swell and fail — never use them with phosphate esters.

materials_qa.q: How do I identify the 8700 back-up ring?
materials_qa.a: It is bright ORANGE — that's its unique identifier. It's a Polymyte (V.PTFE) back-up ring.

materials_qa.q: What is a buffer ring and when is it needed?
materials_qa.a: A buffer ring (e.g. HBY series) absorbs hydraulic pressure spikes before they reach the primary rod seal. Used in high-pressure cylinders where valve switching or load changes create spikes.

materials_qa.q: What are wear rings (guiding elements) for?
materials_qa.a: They absorb radial/side loads, preventing metal-to-metal contact between rod/piston and bore. Without them, off-center loads cause scoring, seal damage, and premature failure.

catalog_reference_system._note: FluidSeal AB Fluid Power Catalog Vol. 6 — section letters before a page number indicate the product family.
catalog_reference_system.example: 'A 2' = AN wiper on page 2 of the Rod Wipers section.

certification_curriculum._note: The 6-level staff certification. Full interactive quiz with scoring + PDF certificate is at destinations.certification_program. Each level's questions and teaching feedback are included so Marion can tutor staff and answer the underlying concepts.

levels.name: Catalog Navigation
levels.focus: How to navigate the FluidSeal AB Fluid Power Catalog Vol. 6 and its A–G section-letter page reference system.

questions.q: The FluidSeal AB main seal catalog is Fluid Power Catalog Volume ___
questions.explanation: The Fluid Power Catalog Vol. 6 covers rod wipers, rod seals, symmetrical seals, piston seals, vee packings, guiding elements, and O-rings.

questions.q: In the catalog page reference system, what does the letter 'A' before the page number indicate?
questions.answer: Rod Wipers
questions.explanation: 'A' = Rod Wipers section. Example: 'A 2' = AN wiper on page 2. B = Rod Seals, C = Symmetrical, D = Piston Seals, E = Vee Packings.

questions.q: What does the catalog section letter 'B' represent?
questions.answer: Rod Seals
questions.explanation: 'B' = Rod Seals. Page references like 'B 5' point to rod seal series pages. This is the second section of the Fluid Power Catalog.

questions.q: The AN rod wiper is found on catalog page:
questions.answer: A 2
questions.explanation: The AN wiper is on catalog page A 2 — section A (Rod Wipers), page 2.

questions.q: Which FluidSeal AB catalog specifically covers seal kits?
questions.answer: Kits Catalog Vol. 2
questions.explanation: The Kits Catalog Vol. 2 covers pre-packaged seal kits.

questions.q: When measuring a U-cup rod seal, what is the CORRECT dimension order for catalog lookup?
questions.answer: c.s. / i.d. / o.d. / hgt.
questions.explanation: For rod seals: c.s. (cross section) first, then i.d., o.d., hgt. This is the universal measurement order for U-cup rod seals.

questions.q: For piston seals, catalog dimension listings always start with:
questions.answer: o.d.
questions.explanation: Piston seals: OD first, then ID, then height. The outer diameter contacts the cylinder bore, so it is the primary identifying dimension.

questions.q: Catalog section letter 'D' represents which product family?
questions.answer: Piston Seals
questions.explanation: D = Piston Seals. Catalog sections: A=Wipers, B=Rod Seals, C=Symmetrical, D=Piston, E=Vee Packing, F=Guiding, G=O-Rings.

questions.q: The abbreviation 'duro.' in the catalog refers to:
questions.answer: Durometer (Shore hardness)
questions.explanation: duro. = Durometer — the Shore A hardness of the elastomer. Harder seals (e.g. 90A) resist extrusion better; softer seals (e.g. 70A) seal better at low pressure.

questions.q: A catalog page reference of 'E 3' belongs to which product category?
questions.answer: Vee Packings
questions.explanation: E = Vee Packings section. E 3 = page 3 of the Vee Packings section.

levels.name: Website Navigation
levels.focus: Finding products on sealsonline.com/en/flabed — categories, inch vs metric, profile search.

questions.q: What is the correct web address for the FluidSeal AB store?
questions.answer: sealsonline.com/en/flabed
questions.explanation: The FluidSeal AB store URL is sealsonline.com/en/flabed — 'en' for English, 'flabed' for the FluidSeal AB Edmonton branch.

questions.q: On sealsonline.com, how are products primarily organized at the top navigation level?
questions.answer: By Inch and Metric standards, then by category
questions.explanation: Products split into Inch and Metric standards first, then by category. This mirrors the catalog structure.

questions.q: To find the metric RA rod seal on sealsonline.com, which URL path applies?
questions.answer: /metric/rod-seals/ra
questions.explanation: RA is a metric rod seal: /metric/rod-seals/ra. Always confirm inch vs metric — wrong standard gives wrong sizes.

questions.q: The AN inch rod wiper URL on sealsonline.com follows the pattern:
questions.answer: /categories/inch/rod-wipers/an
questions.explanation: Full URL: sealsonline.com/en/flabed/categories/inch/rod-wipers/an. Structure: /categories/[standard]/[category]/[series].

questions.q: On the Product Profiles Training Guide, the Quick Reference Table can be filtered by:
questions.answer: Standard (Inch/Metric) and Category
questions.explanation: The QR table has two dropdowns: Standard (Inch/Metric) and Category. Combine with text search to narrow any series.

questions.q: When a product page shows '24 Series' on sealsonline.com, this means:
questions.answer: There are 24 size variants available in that series
questions.explanation: 'X Series' badge = number of individual size variants stocked. '24 Series' = 24 different sizes available.

questions.q: The 'Shop ↗' link on a product card in the training guide takes you to:
questions.answer: The FluidSeal AB store page for that specific series
questions.explanation: Every product card has a 'Shop ↗' link mapped to the exact series URL on sealsonline.com.

questions.q: To quickly navigate between product categories without scrolling, you use:
questions.answer: The sticky category navigation bar
questions.explanation: The sticky category nav bar uses scrollspy to highlight the active section and allows one-click jumps between all 8 categories.

questions.q: On the Quick Reference Table, clicking the product image thumbnail links to:
questions.answer: The product page on sealsonline.com
questions.explanation: Every product image thumbnail in the QR Table is a direct link to that series on sealsonline.com/en/flabed.

questions.q: If SERIES_LINKS has no entry for a series, the training guide defaults to:
questions.answer: Linking to the category page on sealsonline.com, then the main store
questions.explanation: Fallback chain: SERIES_LINKS[series] → CAT_LINKS[category] → sealsonline.com/en/flabed. Every series always has a valid store link.

levels.name: Profiles and Shapes
levels.focus: Identifying seal profiles by cross-section shape and matching them to applications.

questions.q: A U-cup seal gets its name because:
questions.answer: Its cross-sectional profile resembles the letter U
questions.explanation: The U-cup cross section looks like the letter U. The two lips contact the housing and rod, creating the seal.

questions.q: A 'symmetrical' seal can be installed on BOTH rod and piston because:
questions.answer: Lip geometry is identical on both sides — sealing direction doesn't matter
questions.explanation: Symmetrical seals have mirror-image lips — both sides identical, so the seal works on a rod bore or against a cylinder bore.

questions.q: How does a rod wiper differ from a rod seal?
questions.answer: Wipers remove contaminants from the rod on outstroke; rod seals prevent fluid from escaping
questions.explanation: The wiper sits outboard of the rod seal and scrapes dirt/moisture off the rod as it retracts, before it can contaminate the rod seal.

questions.q: The cross-sectional shape of a standard O-ring is:
questions.answer: Circular (round)
questions.explanation: O-rings have a circular (toroidal) cross section. Compression in the groove creates the seal. Quad-Rings have a 4-lobed cross section.

questions.q: A vee packing assembly consists of:
questions.answer: Male adaptor + female adaptor + one or more V-rings stacked between them
questions.explanation: Vee packings: male adaptor at bottom, V-rings in middle (add more for higher pressure), female adaptor at top. Gland nut compresses for adjustment.

questions.q: Piston seals seal against the:
questions.answer: Outside diameter against the cylinder bore
questions.explanation: Piston seals ride the piston OD and seal against the cylinder bore ID. Rod seals sit in the gland and seal against the rod OD.

questions.q: The HBY buffer ring profile is:
questions.answer: A U-cup with an integrated anti-extrusion heel ring
questions.explanation: HBY is a U-cup buffer ring with an AER built into the heel. Absorbs pressure spikes before they reach the primary rod seal.

questions.q: Multi-piece piston seals use PTFE as the outer ring because:
questions.answer: PTFE gives low friction and broad chemical resistance; rubber energizer provides radial load since PTFE lacks elasticity
questions.explanation: PTFE has excellent low friction and chemical resistance but no elastic recovery. The rubber energizer pushes the PTFE cap against the bore for sealing load.

questions.q: The Polypak® seal profile is best described as:
questions.answer: A U-cup with a loaded shallow-lip profile for both rod and piston applications
questions.explanation: Polypak® is a single-piece U-cup with a loaded-lip profile. Standard, Deep, Type B, and Spring-Loaded variants exist.

questions.q: Back-up rings are used alongside O-rings to:
questions.answer: Prevent the O-ring from extruding into the clearance gap under high pressure
questions.explanation: Under high pressure, O-rings flow into clearance gaps and extrude — eventually cutting and failing. Back-up rings close the gap.

questions.q: The PRIMARY purpose of a guiding element / wear ring is:
questions.answer: Absorb radial/side loads and prevent metal-to-metal contact between rod/piston and bore
questions.explanation: Wear rings absorb side loads from machine weight and external forces. Without them, rod/piston contacts bore — causing scoring, seal damage, premature failure.

questions.q: A step-cut outer ring on a piston seal has this geometry to:
questions.answer: Allow the ring to be compressed for installation into the bore
questions.explanation: Step-cut rings can be compressed smaller than nominal OD for fitting into the cylinder bore, then spring back to seal.

questions.q: A Quad-Ring's advantage over an O-ring in dynamic applications is:
questions.answer: 4 sealing contact points and resistance to spiral twist failure in reciprocating applications
questions.explanation: O-rings in dynamic applications can spiral-twist and fail. The Quad-Ring's 4-lobe cross section resists this twist while providing 4 sealing contact points.

questions.q: The DU bearing construction from outside to inside is:
questions.answer: Steel backing + porous bronze layer + PTFE/lead sliding surface
questions.explanation: DU bearings: steel backing → sintered porous bronze layer → PTFE + lead overlay. Never scratch the inside PTFE with caliper points.

questions.q: Why is a buffer ring installed outboard of the primary rod seal?
questions.answer: Absorbs hydraulic pressure spikes before they reach the rod seal, extending primary seal life
questions.explanation: High-pressure spikes fatigue and blow out rod seals. The buffer ring absorbs the spike and allows pressure to equalize gradually before it reaches the primary seal.

levels.name: Materials
levels.focus: Seal materials (PU/AU, NBR, FKM, EPDM, PTFE and fills) and their properties.

questions.q: The most common seal material at FluidSeal AB is:
questions.answer: Polyurethane
questions.explanation: Polyurethane is the primary seal material — outstanding abrasion resistance, tensile strength, and good compatibility with mineral hydraulic oil.

questions.q: What makes Polyurethane preferred for hydraulic rod seals over NBR?
questions.answer: Excellent abrasion resistance (3–5× better than NBR), high tensile strength, and superior extrusion resistance
questions.explanation: PU has dramatically better abrasion resistance than NBR — critical for rod seals exposed to contaminated rods. Higher tensile = better extrusion resistance.

questions.q: The ASTM designation for polyester-based polyurethane is:
questions.answer: AU
questions.explanation: AU = Polyester-based Polyurethane. EU = Polyether-based. AU is common; EU has better hydrolysis resistance for wet environments.

questions.q: Shore A durometer hardness measures:
questions.answer: Hardness of rubber/elastomeric materials
questions.explanation: Shore A = standard hardness scale for soft elastomers. 40A = very soft, 90A+ = very hard. Most hydraulic seals are 70A–95A.

questions.q: Compared to 70A, a 90A seal of the same material is:
questions.answer: Harder, more extrusion resistant, better for high pressure
questions.explanation: Higher durometer = harder, stiffer, better extrusion resistance. 90A = high-pressure applications or larger clearance gaps.

questions.q: Which elastomer must NEVER be used with petroleum hydraulic oil?
questions.answer: EPDM (Ethylene Propylene)
questions.explanation: EPDM swells dramatically in petroleum oils — absorbs oil, loses mechanical properties, fails quickly. EPDM is for water/steam/chemical applications ONLY.

questions.q: FKM (Viton®) is preferred over NBR when:
questions.answer: High temperatures, aggressive chemicals, or aromatic fuels
questions.explanation: FKM excels where standard materials fail: temperatures up to 400°F, aggressive chemicals, aromatic hydrocarbons. High fluorine content = extremely chemically stable.

questions.q: PTFE is used as the outer ring in multi-piece piston seals because:
questions.answer: Very low friction + broad chemical resistance, with rubber energizer compensating for lack of elastic recovery
questions.explanation: PTFE: near-zero friction, resists virtually all chemicals, but not elastic. Rubber O-ring inside provides the spring force to keep PTFE cap against bore.

questions.q: The Parker compound prefix letter for NBR (Nitrile) is:
questions.answer: N
questions.explanation: Parker prefixes: N=NBR, V=FKM, E=EPDM, K=HNBR, S=Silicone, L=Fluorosilicone, F=FFKM, B=IIR. Part number starting with N = Nitrile.

questions.q: Which elastomer has the widest temperature range?
questions.answer: VMQ Silicone (−175 to +450°F)
questions.explanation: Silicone (VMQ): −175°F to +450°F widest range. FFKM reaches +608°F but has narrower lower limit. Silicone for extreme-cold-to-moderate-heat cycling.

questions.q: IIR (Butyl Rubber) is specified for its unique advantage of:
questions.answer: Lowest gas permeability — best for vacuum and gas containment
questions.explanation: Butyl has the lowest gas permeability of any common elastomer — critical for vacuum seals and phosphate ester hydraulic systems. NOT petroleum oil compatible.

questions.q: Bronze-filled PTFE (BR.PTFE) is preferred over Glass-filled PTFE (GF.PTFE) when:
questions.answer: High thermal conductivity and wear resistance for lubricated metallic bore conditions
questions.explanation: BR.PTFE = better thermal conductivity and wear resistance in lubricated conditions. GF.PTFE = better chemical resistance in dry or chemical environments.

questions.q: 'AU 90A' on a seal specification means:
questions.answer: Polyester-based Polyurethane, Shore 90A hardness
questions.explanation: AU = Polyester-based Polyurethane (ASTM AU). 90A = Shore A hardness 90. Standard hydraulic rod seal grade.

questions.q: Nylon (Polyamide / PA) is used in seal assemblies primarily as:
questions.answer: Anti-extrusion rings and wear rings due to stiffness and dimensional stability
questions.explanation: Nylon's stiffness and dimensional stability make it ideal for anti-extrusion rings and wear rings — non-deforming bearing and gap-closing surfaces.

questions.q: AEM (Vamac® / Ethylene Acrylate) compared to ACM (Polyacrylate) offers:
questions.answer: Better low-temperature flexibility to −40°F while retaining heat-in-oil resistance
questions.explanation: ACM: great oil at high temp, poor cold. AEM adds cold flexibility to −40°F while keeping similar oil resistance. Key advantage for cold-climate equipment.

levels.name: Applications and Markets
levels.focus: Matching seals to industries, equipment, and operating conditions.

questions.q: A hydraulic cylinder in a mobile excavator would typically use:
questions.answer: Polyurethane U-cup rod seal + rod wiper for dirt/contaminant protection
questions.explanation: Mobile equipment cylinders use PU rod seals for abrasion resistance and a rod wiper to scrape dirt/mud/ice off the rod before it reaches the rod seal.

questions.q: In a double-acting hydraulic cylinder, where is sealing required?
questions.answer: Both sides of the piston AND on the rod
questions.explanation: Double-acting = powered both ways. Requires: piston seal (both sides), rod seal, rod wiper, and wear rings for lateral load support.

questions.q: For a hydraulic cylinder in food processing, the critical material requirement is:
questions.answer: FDA-approved materials (VMQ silicone, FKM) for incidental food contact
questions.explanation: Food-grade = FDA-compliant materials (21 CFR). FDA-grade silicone, FKM, and specific NBR compounds. Standard seals may contain non-approved carbon black or plasticizers.

questions.q: FFKM (Perfluoroelastomer) seals are specified for:
questions.answer: Semiconductor plasma etching with aggressive process chemicals
questions.explanation: FFKM = most chemically resistant elastomer. Used where all others fail: semiconductor fabs, aggressive pharma CIP/SIP, high-temp chemical reactors. Cost 10–50× standard FKM.

questions.q: Vee packing seals are best suited for:
questions.answer: Slow-speed reciprocating high-pressure applications where gland adjustment is possible
questions.explanation: Vee packings excel in slow, high-pressure reciprocating service (presses, jacks). Adjustable gland compensates for wear. Not suited for fast-cycling unattended applications.

questions.q: A buffer ring (HBY) is installed to:
questions.answer: Absorb pressure spikes before they reach the rod seal, extending primary seal life
questions.explanation: High-pressure spikes from fast valve switching or sudden load changes fatigue rod seals. Buffer ring absorbs the spike, allows pressure to equalize gradually.

questions.q: For a cylinder in Arctic conditions below −40°F, specify:
questions.answer: Low-temperature FKM compound or Fluorosilicone (FVMQ) rated to −100°F
questions.explanation: Standard PU and NBR become brittle below −40°F. FVMQ goes to −100°F. Low-temp FKM compounds also handle Arctic conditions while maintaining chemical resistance.

questions.q: Wear rings in a hydraulic cylinder primarily manage:
questions.answer: Radial/lateral loads on rod and piston, preventing metal-to-metal contact
questions.explanation: Without wear rings, off-center loads from machine weight or tooling cause rod/piston to contact bore — scoring, seal damage, rapid failure.

questions.q: Pneumatic cylinders differ from hydraulic in that they require:
questions.answer: Low-friction seals for compressed air (lower pressure, cleaner environment), less emphasis on extrusion resistance
questions.explanation: Pneumatic: compressed air, typically 8–12 bar, clean environment, low friction priority. Hydraulic: oil, 150–350 bar, contaminated environment, extrusion resistance priority.

questions.q: The largest volume user of hydraulic cylinder seals in North America is:
questions.answer: Mobile equipment (construction, agriculture, forestry, mining)
questions.explanation: Mobile equipment is by far the largest seal market: excavators, loaders, agricultural equipment, forestry machines, and mining all use extensive hydraulic cylinder systems.

questions.q: For a forestry mulcher in extreme abrasion, the ideal seal specification prioritizes:
questions.answer: High durometer polyurethane with multi-lip wiper for bark, rocks, debris
questions.explanation: Forestry: extreme abrasion from bark/rocks/wire. Hard PU (90A+) for extrusion resistance; multi-lip wiper (H-series or BW/S metal wiper) to remove aggressive contaminants.

questions.q: A hydraulic press at 420 bar (6000 PSI) needs:
questions.answer: Multi-piece seal with PTFE cap + AER anti-extrusion rings + elastomeric energizer
questions.explanation: At very high pressure, single-piece seals extrude. Multi-piece seals (P4A, CTS, CAT-TYPE-4) combine PTFE sealing with AERs to handle extreme pressures reliably.

questions.q: Rotary shaft sealing requires different seal types than reciprocating seals because:
questions.answer: Rotary requires lip seals for continuous 360° contact at speed, not U-cups designed for linear stroke
questions.explanation: U-cup seals cannot handle continuous rotation — the lip would wear quickly and generate heat. Rotary shaft seals have spring-loaded continuous lip geometry optimized for speed.

questions.q: A cylinder sitting overnight at −30°F fails on first stroke. Most likely cause:
questions.answer: Rubber seals below glass transition temperature become brittle and crack on dynamic shock loading
questions.explanation: Elastomers below glass transition temperature (Tg) become rigid and brittle. First-stroke dynamic loads crack a brittle seal — causing immediate leakage. Low-temp compounds prevent this.

questions.q: A cylinder on equipment spraying organophosphate herbicides requires:
questions.answer: FKM or EPDM evaluated based on specific chemical compatibility — standard PU and NBR may swell
questions.explanation: Organophosphate herbicides attack standard PU and NBR. FKM has broad chemical resistance; EPDM resists many polar chemicals. Always check Parker Chemical Resistance Guide.

levels.name: Material Science and Chemical Compatibility
levels.focus: Polymer science — compression set, Tg, chemical attack, fluid compatibility.

questions.q: What is 'compression set' and why does it matter?
questions.answer: Permanent deformation after sustained compression — high compression set means the seal loses return force and eventually leaks
questions.explanation: Compression set = % of original deflection that becomes permanent. 100% compression set = flat seal, no longer springs back against mating surface. Low compression set = longer seal life.

questions.q: The 'glass transition temperature' (Tg) is significant because:
questions.answer: Below Tg, the elastomer becomes rigid and brittle, losing all sealing function
questions.explanation: Below Tg, molecular chain mobility stops — material becomes hard and brittle like glass. Seals operating below Tg crack on dynamic loading. Low-Tg compounds specified for cold climates.

questions.q: Why does NBR fail with ketone solvents (acetone, MEK)?
questions.answer: Ketones are polar solvents that swell and plasticize NBR, causing rapid loss of mechanical properties
questions.explanation: Ketones are polar solvents strongly attracted to nitrile groups in NBR. They penetrate the polymer matrix, causing severe swelling, softening, and dissolution of the seal.

questions.q: EPDM fails rapidly in petroleum hydraulic oil because:
questions.answer: Petroleum hydrocarbons cause severe swelling — seal absorbs oil, expands, loses all mechanical integrity
questions.explanation: EPDM and petroleum hydrocarbons are incompatible. Non-polar hydrocarbons swell non-polar EPDM. Volume swell can reach 100%+ — the seal becomes shapeless.

questions.q: Increasing acrylonitrile (ACN) content in NBR has what effect?
questions.answer: Better oil resistance but worse cold-temperature flexibility (higher Tg)
questions.explanation: ACN content spectrum: low ACN (18-22%) = flexible at cold, moderate oil resistance. High ACN (40-50%) = excellent oil/fuel resistance, poor cold flex. Standard grade ~33-36% ACN.

questions.q: FKM's exceptional chemical resistance comes from:
questions.answer: High fluorine content and extremely stable C-F bonds (among the strongest bonds in organic chemistry)
questions.explanation: C-F bond dissociation energy ~544 kJ/mol vs C-H ~413 kJ/mol. Fluorine's high electronegativity creates a protective shield around the carbon backbone.

questions.q: Volume swell in a seal material — is it always negative?
questions.answer: No — slight swell (3-8%) can tighten seal-to-groove fit and improve sealing; excess swell causes extrusion, leakage, and groove lock-up
questions.explanation: Slight volume swell is often engineered in — a seal in service swells slightly in oil, increasing interference fit. Problems above ~15-20% swell where seal extrudes or sticks in groove.

questions.q: H₂S (hydrogen sulfide / sour gas) environments require HNBR because:
questions.answer: Hydrogenation removes C=C double bonds that H₂S attacks — HNBR resists sour gas where standard NBR fails
questions.explanation: H₂S attacks residual double bonds (C=C) in NBR through sulfide attack. Hydrogenating NBR (HNBR) saturates these bonds — drastically improving resistance to H₂S, ozone, and heat.

questions.q: Phosphate ester hydraulic fluids (Skydrol, Fyrquel) require which elastomers?
questions.answer: IIR (Butyl) and EPDM
questions.explanation: Phosphate esters destroy NBR and polyurethane quickly. IIR (Butyl) and EPDM both resist phosphate esters. Critical for aerospace and fire-resistant hydraulic systems.

questions.q: Ozone cracking occurs because ozone attacks:
questions.answer: C=C double bonds in unsaturated elastomers — NBR, NR, SBR most susceptible; FKM, EPDM, VMQ resistant
questions.explanation: O₃ is highly reactive with C=C double bonds — cleaves them causing surface cracking perpendicular to stress. EPDM has very few C=C bonds. FKM has none.

questions.q: Biodiesel (B100/high-blend) causes what concern for seals?
questions.answer: Severe swelling/degradation of standard NBR and polyurethane; high-ACN NBR or FKM recommended
questions.explanation: Biodiesel contains ester compounds and free fatty acids that attack standard NBR and PU. FKM is safest; high-ACN NBR has improved but not complete resistance.

questions.q: A seal fails with cracked, hard surface after 6 months. This indicates:
questions.answer: Thermal oxidative degradation — material exceeded continuous temperature rating causing chain scission and hardening
questions.explanation: Over-temperature causes oxidative degradation: polymer chains break (scission), material loses plasticizers, becomes hard and brittle, finally cracks. Surface hardening + cracking = thermal signature.

questions.q: Higher crosslink density in vulcanized rubber means:
questions.answer: Harder material with better compression set resistance but reduced elongation
questions.explanation: More crosslinks = stiffer network = harder, less flexible, better compression set. Fewer crosslinks = more flexible, better elongation, worse compression set.

questions.q: Hot water above 60°C causes polyurethane seal failure because:
questions.answer: Water hydrolyzes urethane linkages — chemically breaking the polymer backbone, causing softening, swelling, disintegration (worst for polyester AU)
questions.explanation: Hydrolysis = water splitting ester/ether linkages in the polymer chain. Polyester AU most vulnerable. Polyether EU resists hydrolysis better. EPDM is correct for hot water service.

questions.q: Fluorosilicone (FVMQ) improves on standard Silicone (VMQ) by providing:
questions.answer: Fuel and aliphatic hydrocarbon resistance that standard silicone lacks, while retaining wide temperature range to −100°F
questions.explanation: Standard VMQ: poor resistance to petroleum oils/fuels. FVMQ adds fluorinated side chains for fuel/oil resistance, keeping silicone's exceptional low-temperature flexibility. Used in aerospace fuel systems.

questions.q: When a seal SHRINKS after fluid exposure, this indicates:
questions.answer: Fluid is extracting plasticizers — hardening and shrinkage cause loss of sealing contact force
questions.explanation: Plasticizer extraction (aggressive solvents or non-polar fluids) hardens and shrinks the elastomer. Seal loses interference fit with mating surfaces and leaks without visible damage.

questions.q: Water-glycol hydraulic fluid (HF-C) requires:
questions.answer: EPDM for water-glycol compatibility, paired with PTFE-based wear rings
questions.explanation: Water-glycol is water-based — EPDM compatible. NBR and PU swell severely. PTFE wear rings for low friction. Standard for fire-resistant water-glycol hydraulic systems.

questions.q: The ASTM test standard for compression set in rubber is:
questions.answer: ASTM D395 (compression set)
questions.explanation: ASTM D395 Method B: compress 25% at specified temperature for 22 or 70 hours, release, measure permanent set %. Below 20% generally acceptable for dynamic seals.

questions.q: Higher molecular weight in an elastomer generally results in:
questions.answer: Better mechanical properties (higher tensile, elongation, tear) from longer polymer chains that entangle more effectively
questions.explanation: Longer polymer chains = more chain entanglements = higher mechanical properties. High-MW NBR or FKM grades show better tensile and tear resistance from physical crosslinks.

questions.q: The Parker ORD 5712 Material Offering Guide is used to:
questions.answer: Identify the appropriate Parker compound for a specific chemical, temperature, and application
questions.explanation: ORD 5712 is Parker's comprehensive material/compound selector: lists every compound with chemical resistance ratings, temperature ranges, hardness, and application guidelines — the definitive material selection reference.
